docs.title: What are Guardrails?
docs.text:
Answer

Guardrails are safety, validation and control mechanisms that regulate AI system behavior before, during and after LLM execution. Their objective is to improve safety, reliability and compliance rather than the intelligence of the model.

docs.title: Do Guardrails make the LLM smarter?
docs.text:
No.

Guardrails improve system reliability, not model intelligence.

docs.title: What are the four major Guardrails?
docs.text:
• Input Guardrails
  • Output Guardrails
  • Tool Guardrails
  • Human Approval (Human-in-the-Loop)

docs.title: What is Prompt Injection?
docs.text:
A malicious attempt to manipulate or override the model's instructions.

Input Guardrails detect and block such attacks.

docs.title: What is Hallucination?
docs.text:
When an LLM generates information that is unsupported or factually incorrect.

Output Guardrails help detect and reduce hallucinations.

docs.title: Why are Tool Guardrails necessary?
docs.text:
Because LLMs may:

  • Choose the wrong tool
  • Generate unsafe arguments
  • Execute unauthorized actions

Tool Guardrails validate all three.

docs.title: What is RBAC?
docs.text:
Role-Based Access Control.

It ensures users (and AI agents acting on their behalf) can access only the tools and resources permitted for their role.

docs.title: When should Human-in-the-Loop be used?
docs.text:
For:

  • Financial transactions
  • Medical decisions
  • Legal approvals
  • High-risk workflows
  • Low-confidence outputs

docs.title: Can Guardrails be rule-based?
docs.text:
Yes.

Regex, schemas, keywords and business rules are common deterministic guardrails.

docs.title: Can another LLM act as a Guardrail?
docs.text:
Yes.

A lightweight LLM can classify prompts, detect unsafe inputs, moderate outputs and validate policy compliance before the main model responds.

docs.title: Guardrails vs Prompt Engineering?
docs.text:
Prompt engineering guides the model.

Guardrails enforce system constraints externally.

docs.title: Can Guardrails eliminate hallucinations completely?
docs.text:
No.

They significantly reduce risk but cannot guarantee perfect correctness.

docs.title: Common Interview Traps
docs.text:
❌ Guardrails are only used in RAG.

✅ Incorrect.

They apply to every AI application.

❌ Prompt Engineering is enough.

✅ Incorrect.

Prompts guide behavior.

Guardrails enforce behavior.

❌ Guardrails eliminate hallucinations.

✅ Incorrect.

They reduce risk.

❌ Human Approval means the AI failed.

✅ Incorrect.

It is an intentional safety mechanism.

❌ Tool Guardrails only check permissions.

✅ Incorrect.

They validate:

  • Tool selection
  • Parameters
  • Permissions
  • Business rules

docs.title: What You Should Remember Forever
docs.text:
Guardrails make **systems safer**, not **models smarter**.

Input Guardrails

↓

Protect the model.

Output Guardrails

↓

Protect the user.

Tool Guardrails

↓

Protect external systems.

Human Approval

↓

Protect high-risk workflows.

Programmatic Guardrails

↓

Fast

Cheap

Deterministic

Model-Based Guardrails

↓

Semantic

Smarter

Costlier

Production AI uses multiple guardrails together, not just one.

  • [x] Common production use cases
  • [x] Common interview questions

If you can explain these confidently without notes, you've mastered the Guardrails topic for Stage 1.

docs.title: Crisp Definition
docs.text:
Structured Output is a technique that forces an LLM to return responses that conform to a predefined schema instead of free-form natural language.

The schema may be defined using:

  • JSON Schema
  • Pydantic Models
  • Dataclasses
  • Typed Objects

Instead of receiving:

The vendor is Amazon.
Invoice date is 15 July.
Amount is ₹1,250.

we receive:

{
  "vendor": "Amazon",
  "invoice_date": "2026-07-15",
  "amount": 1250
}

The output becomes directly usable by software.

docs.title: Why Do We Need Structured Outputs?
docs.text:
LLMs naturally generate text.

Software expects structured data.

Without structured outputs:

LLM

↓

Random Text

↓

Developer writes fragile parsing logic

With structured outputs:

LLM

↓

Validated JSON

↓

Database

↓

API

↓

Business Logic

No unreliable string parsing.

docs.title: Predictability
docs.text:
Applications always receive data in a known format.

Example

{
    "vendor_name": "...",
    "invoice_date": "...",
    "total_amount": ...
}

instead of paragraphs.

docs.title: Reliability
docs.text:
Modern LLM APIs support schema-constrained generation.

If the model cannot satisfy the schema,

↓

validation fails

↓

application retries or handles the error.

docs.title: Type Safety
docs.text:
Each field has a defined data type.

Example

amount: float

instead of

"One Thousand Rupees"

docs.title: Easier API Integration
docs.text:
Structured output can directly populate

  • REST APIs
  • Databases
  • Event Queues
  • Microservices

docs.title: Source Traceability
docs.text:
Schemas can include

  • document IDs
  • citations
  • confidence scores
  • page numbers

Example

{
  "answer": "...",
  "source": "page_15.pdf",
  "confidence": 0.94
}

Very useful in RAG.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Pydantic is a Python library for defining, validating and enforcing structured data models.

Instead of trusting an LLM,

Pydantic verifies:

  • required fields
  • data types
  • constraints

before your application uses the response.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Invoice(BaseModel):
    vendor: str
    amount: float
    invoice_date: str

Now the LLM must produce something matching:

{
    "vendor":"Amazon",
    "amount":1250,
    "invoice_date":"2026-07-15"
}

docs.title: Step 1
docs.text:
Developer defines

class Invoice(BaseModel):

↓

docs.title: Step 2
docs.text:
Pydantic converts it into

JSON Schema

↓

docs.title: Step 3
docs.text:
Framework (LangChain/OpenAI/etc.)

adds instructions like

Return ONLY valid JSON matching this schema.

↓

docs.title: Step 4
docs.text:
LLM generates

JSON

↓

docs.title: Step 5
docs.text:
Pydantic validates

↓

Success

↓

Python Object

OR

↓

Validation Error

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
JSON Schema is a standard way of describing the expected structure of JSON data.

Example

{
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
      "vendor": {
          "type": "string"
      },
      "amount": {
          "type": "number"
      }
  }
}

Most frameworks generate this automatically from Pydantic models.

docs.title: JSON Repair
docs.text:
LLMs sometimes generate

{
"name":"John",
}

or

{
...
}

These are invalid.

Applications often

  • remove markdown
  • fix commas
  • repair brackets
  • retry generation

before parsing.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
An Output Parser is responsible for converting raw LLM output into usable structured data.

Think of it as the bridge between

LLM

↓

Application

docs.title: Prompt Injection
docs.text:
Adds formatting instructions automatically.

Example

Return ONLY valid JSON.

docs.title: Parsing
docs.text:
Converts

{
...
}

↓

Python Object

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Uses

Pydantic

↓

Check

  • Required fields
  • Types
  • Constraints

docs.title: Repair
docs.text:
Some parsers automatically

  • retry
  • repair JSON
  • ask the LLM to regenerate

if parsing fails.

docs.title: Overall Architecture
docs.text:
Pydantic Model
               │
               ▼
        JSON Schema
               │
               ▼
        Output Parser
               │
               ▼
    Prompt Instructions
               │
               ▼
             LLM
               │
               ▼
        Raw JSON/Text
               │
               ▼
        Output Parser
               │
        Parse + Validate
               │
               ▼
        Python Object
               │
               ▼
         Application

docs.title: Pydantic vs JSON vs Output Parser
docs.text:
Pydantic JSON Output Parser Defines schema Data format Converts LLM output Validates data Stores data Parses & validates Python library Universal format Framework component

Easy way to remember:

Pydantic defines.

JSON transports.

Output Parser converts and validates.

docs.title: Structured Outputs in RAG
docs.text:
Instead of

LLM

↓

Answer

RAG applications often return

{
  "answer":"...",
  "source":"doc1.pdf",
  "page":18,
  "confidence":0.92
}

This allows applications to

  • display citations
  • audit responses
  • verify sources
  • integrate with downstream APIs

docs.title: What are Structured Outputs?
docs.text: Structured outputs force an LLM to generate responses that conform to a predefined schema such as JSON or a Pydantic model, making the output reliable for software systems.

docs.title: Why are Structured Outputs needed?
docs.text: Because LLMs naturally produce free-form text, while applications require predictable, machine-readable data.

docs.title: What is Pydantic?
docs.text: Pydantic is a Python library that defines and validates structured data models, ensuring the LLM output satisfies the expected schema before it is used.

docs.title: What is JSON Schema?
docs.text: JSON Schema is a specification that describes the structure, required fields and data types of JSON documents.

docs.title: Does the LLM understand Python?
docs.text:
Not directly.

Frameworks convert Pydantic models into JSON Schema, which is then used to guide generation.

docs.title: What happens if validation fails?
docs.text:
The application can:

  • retry generation
  • repair JSON
  • return an error
  • ask the LLM to regenerate

docs.title: What does an Output Parser do?
docs.text: It injects formatting instructions, parses the response, validates it and may repair malformed outputs.

docs.title: Why not parse text manually?
docs.text:
Manual parsing is fragile.

Small wording changes can break the application.

Structured outputs provide a stable contract.

docs.title: Can Structured Outputs eliminate hallucinations?
docs.text:
No.

They guarantee structure, not truthfulness.

A perfectly structured JSON response can still contain incorrect facts.

docs.title: Why are Structured Outputs useful in RAG?
docs.text: They allow answers to include structured citations, confidence scores, document IDs and other metadata that downstream systems can consume.

docs.title: Common Interview Traps
docs.text:
❌ Structured Output guarantees factual correctness.

✔ Incorrect.

It guarantees format, not correctness.

❌ JSON and Pydantic are the same.

✔ JSON is a data format.

Pydantic is a Python validation library.

❌ Output Parser is Pydantic.

✔ Output Parser uses Pydantic but also injects prompts, parses and validates.

❌ LLM returns Python objects.

✔ LLM returns text.

Frameworks convert that text into Python objects.

docs.title: Remember These Forever
docs.text:
Structured Output

↓

Predictable Responses

Pydantic

↓

Defines & validates schema

JSON

↓

Data exchange format

Output Parser

↓

Prompts

↓

Parses

↓

Validates

↓

Repairs

Structured Output guarantees

format

NOT

truthfulness

Most modern GenAI applications use Structured Outputs for:

  • APIs
  • Databases
  • Agents
  • Tool Calling
  • Enterprise Workflows

docs.text:

  • Structured Outputs

docs.title: ⭐ One interview insight that almost no tutorial mentions
docs.text:
People often confuse Structured Outputs, Function Calling, and Tool Calling.

Think of them like this:

Feature Purpose Structured Output Force the LLM to return data in a predefined format (JSON, Pydantic, etc.). Function Calling Let the LLM decide which predefined function to invoke and with what arguments. Tool Calling A broader concept where the LLM can interact with external capabilities (functions, APIs, databases, calculators, search, etc.).

An easy mental model is:

Structured Output
        │
        ▼
Produces clean data

Function Calling
        │
        ▼
Chooses which function to execute

Tool Calling
        │
        ▼
Interacts with external systems

Many engineers mix these up in interviews. Being able to clearly distinguish them is a strong signal that you understand modern AI application architectures rather than just individual libraries.

docs.title: Thinking Like a Senior Engineer.v1
docs.text:
These notes are not about AI or LangChain.

They are lessons learned while designing the Knowledge Assistant project.

The goal is to learn how experienced engineers think before writing code.

docs.title: Principle 1 — Design Before Code
docs.text:
Instead of immediately writing code, ask:

  • What problem am I solving?
  • What responsibilities exist?
  • Which components should own those responsibilities?
  • Can this design scale in the future?

Good software is designed first and implemented second.

docs.title: Goal
docs.text:
Create a clean project foundation.

Not a RAG system.

Not a polished SaaS.

Simply a project that another engineer can clone and immediately understand.

docs.title: Why use separate modules instead of writing everything inside app.py?
My Answer
docs.text:
Separation of Concerns.

The architecture should be plug-and-play.

Each module exposes a contract, allowing implementations to change without affecting other modules.

Example

Today

FAISS

↓

Tomorrow

Qdrant

Only the implementation changes.

The rest of the application remains unaffected.

docs.title: Better Interview Answer
docs.text:
Each module has a single responsibility and exposes a clear contract.

This allows implementations to be replaced (e.g., FAISS → Qdrant) without affecting the orchestration layer.

The application depends on abstractions rather than concrete implementations.

docs.title: If we replace FAISS with Qdrant, how many files should change?
My Initial Answer
docs.text:
embeddings.py

vectorstore.py

docs.title: Discussion
docs.text:
Actually,

only

vectorstore.py

should change.

The embedding model has no knowledge of where vectors are stored.

Its responsibility is simply

Text

↓

Embedding Vector

Storage is someone else's responsibility.

This is true decoupling.

docs.title: Lesson
docs.text: Never let one module know unnecessary implementation details about another.

docs.title: Why create many small Pull Requests instead of one huge PR?
My Answer
docs.text:
Small PRs provide

  • easier collaboration
  • manageable reviews
  • better testing
  • incremental delivery
  • quicker feedback
  • sprint-based development

docs.title: Additional Lesson
docs.text:
Imagine

7000 changed lines

versus

PR-09

↓

Introduced Guardrails

If a bug appears,

small PRs are

  • easier to review
  • easier to rollback
  • easier to debug

docs.title: Lesson
docs.text:
Each PR should represent one engineering milestone.

Not simply "more code."

docs.title: Module Responsibilities
docs.text:
Initial design

loaders.py
→ Load documents

splitter.py
→ Split documents

embeddings.py
→ Generate embeddings

vectorstore.py
→ Store and retrieve vectors

rag.py
→ Orchestrate everything

docs.title: Discussion
docs.text: Uploading documents and answering questions are two completely different workflows.

docs.title: Workflow 1
docs.text:
Knowledge Ingestion

PDF

↓

Loader

↓

Splitter

↓

Embeddings

↓

Vector Store

docs.title: Workflow 2
docs.text:
Question Answering

Question

↓

Retriever

↓

Prompt

↓

LLM

↓

Answer

docs.title: Future Problem
docs.text:
Today

loaders.py

supports

PDF

Tomorrow

  • PDF
  • Word
  • Markdown
  • Website
  • PowerPoint

Should we keep adding

if pdf

elif docx

elif md

elif website

?

Probably not.

docs.title: My Initial Idea
docs.text:
Use

  • Parent class
  • Child classes
  • Inversion of Control

The frontend determines the appropriate loader and passes it to the backend.

docs.title: Discussion
docs.text:
The frontend should never know backend implementation details.

Instead,

the frontend simply uploads

resume.pdf

The backend decides

Extension

↓

LoaderFactory

↓

PdfLoader

↓

load()

The frontend only knows

Upload File

Everything else is backend responsibility.

docs.title: Better Design
docs.text:
DocumentLoader
                         ▲
                         │
        ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐
        ▼                ▼                ▼
   PdfLoader      DocxLoader      WebLoader
                         ▲
                         │
                  LoaderFactory
                         ▲
                         │
                  Uploaded File

docs.title: Design Principles Accidentally Learned
docs.text:
Without studying design patterns, this discussion naturally introduced

  • Separation of Concerns
  • Single Responsibility Principle
  • Dependency Inversion Thinking
  • Open/Closed Principle
  • Polymorphism
  • Factory Pattern (conceptually)

This reinforced the idea that project-driven learning teaches design patterns naturally.

docs.title: Important Lesson
docs.text: Don't memorize design patterns. Build enough projects and you'll rediscover them yourself. Later, when learning the official pattern names, they become recognition instead of memorization.

docs.title: Interview Insight
docs.text:
Question

Why use a Factory instead of writing

if extension == "pdf"

everywhere?

Answer

A Factory centralizes object creation.

Adding support for a new document type only requires creating a new implementation and registering it with the factory, rather than modifying multiple parts of the application.

This follows the Open/Closed Principle.

docs.title: Homework
docs.text:
Think about the following architecture.

                  Loader
                    ▲
      ┌─────────────┼─────────────┐
      ▼             ▼             ▼
 PdfLoader     DocxLoader     WebLoader

Without writing code, answer the following questions.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
What methods should the parent Loader define?

Should it expose only

load()

or should it define additional responsibilities?

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Should LoaderFactory return

PdfLoader

or

Loader

Why?

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Tomorrow we add

ExcelLoader

How many existing files should require modification?

If your answer is

One registration point

or close to it,

your architecture is becoming extensible.

docs.title: Biggest Takeaway
docs.text:
Before writing code, always ask

"If the requirements change tomorrow, how much of my code will I need to rewrite?"

The best software is not the one that works today.

The best software is the one that is easiest to change tomorrow.

docs.title: PR-2 Discussion — Designing an Extensible Loader Architecture
docs.text: These answers are based on engineering principles such as SOLID, Separation of Concerns, and Open/Closed Principle, rather than simply making the code "work."

docs.title: What methods should the parent Loader define?
docs.text:
Should it expose only

load()

or should it define additional responsibilities?

docs.title: Initial Thought
docs.text:
At first glance, it seems that every loader only needs one method:

load()

After all,

  • PDF Loader loads PDFs
  • Word Loader loads Word documents
  • Markdown Loader loads Markdown files

All of them ultimately return LangChain Document objects.

docs.title: Better Design
docs.text:
The parent class should define only the behavior that every loader is guaranteed to support.

For our current project, that is simply

load()

Example

Loader
│
├── load()
│
├── PdfLoader
├── DocxLoader
├── MarkdownLoader
└── WebLoader

Every child loader knows how to load its own document type.

docs.title: Why not define more methods?
docs.text:
Suppose we also define

validate()

extract_images()

extract_tables()

Immediately we have a problem.

A WebLoader may not support image extraction.

A MarkdownLoader may not contain tables.

A TXTLoader may not require validation.

Now every child class is forced to implement methods that don't make sense.

This violates the Interface Segregation Principle (ISP).

docs.title: Engineering Principle
docs.text:
A parent class should define only the common contract shared by every implementation.

If every loader can only guarantee one operation,

then one operation is enough.

Don't force child classes to implement methods they don't need.

docs.title: What Should LoaderFactory return
docs.text:
PdfLoader

or

Loader

Why?

docs.title: Correct Answer
docs.text:
The Factory should always return

Loader

(the parent type)

not

PdfLoader

docs.title: ❌ Returning Concrete Classes
docs.text:
Uploaded File
                      │
                      ▼
              LoaderFactory.create()
                      │
          ┌───────────┴───────────┐
          ▼                       ▼
    PdfLoader               DocxLoader
          │                       │
          ▼                       ▼
      Client Code          Client Code

Now the client needs to know:

if isinstance(loader, PdfLoader):
    loader.load()

elif isinstance(loader, DocxLoader):
    loader.load()

The client is tightly coupled to every implementation.

Every time a new loader is introduced, the client code must change.

docs.title: ✅ Returning the Parent Type
docs.text:
Uploaded File
                      │
                      ▼
              LoaderFactory.create()
                      │
                      ▼
                 Loader (Reference)
                      │
      ┌───────────────┼────────────────┐
      ▼               ▼                ▼
 PdfLoader      DocxLoader      ExcelLoader
      │               │                │
      └───────────────┴────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼
                  load()

The client never knows the actual implementation.

It simply writes

loader = LoaderFactory.create(file)
documents = loader.load()

without knowing the concrete implementation.

docs.title: Why is this important?
docs.text:
Suppose tomorrow we replace/add

PdfLoader

with

BetterPdfLoader

Does the client code change?

No.

Does the Factory change?

Only enough to know about the new loader
(or not even that if loaders are auto-registered).

This is why we program to abstractions, not implementations.

docs.title: Engineering Principle
docs.text:
Depend on abstractions, not concrete classes.

This is one of the core ideas behind the Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP).

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Tomorrow we add

ExcelLoader

How many existing files should require modification?

docs.title: Ideal Answer
docs.text: As close to one file as possible.

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
If adding a new document type requires modifying

  • loader.py
  • rag.py
  • app.py
  • splitter.py
  • embeddings.py

then the architecture is tightly coupled.

Instead,

adding a new feature should mostly involve

  1. Creating a new implementation
ExcelLoader
  1. Registering it with the Factory

Everything else should continue working.

docs.title: Ideal Workflow
docs.text:
Before

Loader
│
├── PdfLoader
├── DocxLoader
└── MarkdownLoader

Tomorrow

Loader
│
├── PdfLoader
├── DocxLoader
├── MarkdownLoader
└── ExcelLoader

The rest of the system remains untouched.

docs.title: Why is this valuable?
docs.text:
Imagine six months from now.

The product supports

  • PDF
  • Word
  • Markdown
  • PowerPoint
  • Excel
  • CSV
  • HTML
  • Websites

If every new loader requires changing five existing files, the project becomes difficult to maintain.

Instead,

each new feature should be

Add a class. Register it. Done.

docs.title: Engineering Principle
docs.text:
"This follows the Open/Closed Principle

Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification.

We extend the application by adding new classes, not by constantly modifying existing ones.

docs.title: Visual Architecture
docs.text:
Loader (Abstract)

                         ▲
                         │
      ┌──────────────────┼──────────────────┐
      ▼                  ▼                  ▼
 PdfLoader         DocxLoader        MarkdownLoader
                                              │
                                              ▼
                                        ExcelLoader
                                              ▲
                                              │
                                       LoaderFactory
                                              ▲
                                              │
                                       Uploaded File

The application communicates only with the parent type. Every concrete loader is hidden behind the Factory.

docs.title: Why should the parent Loader expose only load()?
docs.text: Because it should define only the behavior common to every loader. Adding unnecessary methods forces child classes to implement responsibilities they may not support, violating the Interface Segregation Principle.

docs.title: Why should the Factory return Loader instead of PdfLoader?
docs.text: Because client code should depend on abstractions rather than concrete implementations. This makes the system loosely coupled and easier to extend.

docs.title: How many files should change when adding ExcelLoader?
docs.text:
Ideally:

  • One new class (ExcelLoader)
  • One registration/update in the Factory (or even zero if using auto-discovery)

Everything else should continue working without modification.

docs.title: Biggest Lesson
docs.text:
The goal of good architecture is not to reduce the amount of code.

The goal is to reduce the amount of existing code that must change when requirements evolve.

The easiest code to maintain is the code you don't have to touch.

docs.title: PR-3 Discussion - Chunking Engine
docs.text:
Note

These notes capture the engineering discussions, design decisions, interview answers, and architectural reasoning behind the Chunking Engine implementation.

docs.title: PR-2
docs.text:
PDF
    │
    ▼
Loader
    │
    ▼
List<Document>

docs.title: PR-3
docs.text:
PDF
    │
    ▼
Loader
    │
    ▼
List<Document>
    │
    ▼
Chunking Engine
    │
    ▼
List<Document> (Chunks)

Notice something important:

The pipeline grows. We never modify app.py. Instead, ingestion.py orchestrates additional stages over time.

docs.title: Should app.py know about Chunking?
docs.text:
No. app.py should remain completely unaware of the internal pipeline.

Instead it should simply call

documents = ingest_documents(uploaded_files)

Today

Load

Tomorrow

Load
↓

Chunk

Later

Load
↓

Chunk
↓

Embed

Eventually

Load
↓

Chunk
↓

Embed
↓

Vector Store

The caller never changes. This is one of the biggest responsibilities of an orchestration layer.

docs.title: Why have an Ingestion Pipeline?
docs.text:
Think of it as a workflow orchestrator.

app.py

↓

"I have files."

↓

ingestion.py

↓

"I know exactly how to process them."

The orchestration layer coordinates all stages while keeping the UI completely decoupled from implementation details.

docs.title: Should we use Strategy Pattern for Chunking?
Initial Thought
docs.text:
We considered creating

Splitter (Abstract)

        ▲

RecursiveSplitter

so different splitting algorithms could be swapped without affecting the rest of the application.

Possible future strategies

  • RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
  • MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter
  • PythonCodeTextSplitter
  • HTMLHeaderTextSplitter
  • SemanticChunker

All share the same goal:

List<Document>

↓

Chunks

Only the algorithm changes. This is the Strategy Pattern.

docs.title: Why NOT use Strategy today?
docs.text:
Current project supports only

RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

There is no runtime decision. Adding an abstraction now would increase complexity without solving an existing problem.

Instead we follow

Design for extension. Implement for today's requirements.

Current implementation

splitter.py

↓

split_documents(documents)

Later, if multiple chunking algorithms are supported, refactor to Strategy Pattern.

docs.title: Factory vs Strategy
docs.text:
Factory answers

Which object should I create?

Example

UploadedFile

↓

LoaderFactory

↓

PdfLoader

Strategy answers

Which algorithm should I execute?

Example

Documents

↓

Chunk Strategy

↓

Recursive Splitter

Factory creates objects. Strategy chooses algorithms.

docs.title: Why RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter?
docs.text:
Many developers think it simply splits by character count. It actually tries to preserve semantic boundaries.

Order of separators

Paragraph (\n\n)

↓

Line (\n)

↓

Space (" ")

↓

Character

Only when larger boundaries fail does it move to smaller ones. Therefore it minimizes broken sentences and incomplete thoughts.

docs.title: Why not split by Pages?
docs.text:
Initially page splitting appears attractive because pages usually contain related information. However pages are presentation boundaries, not semantic boundaries.

Example

Page 17

Spring Boot provides dependency...

-------------------------

Page 18

...Injection and IoC...

Now one logical concept spans two pages.

Retrieval quality decreases.

Similarly

  • One page may contain many unrelated topics.
  • One topic may span multiple pages.

Therefore page boundaries should not define chunk boundaries.

docs.title: Why Chunk at all?
docs.text:
Embedding the entire document creates a single semantic representation.

Problems

  • Retrieval becomes coarse.
  • Embedding represents an "average meaning" of the document.
  • Context window may overflow.
  • Entire document must be sent to the LLM.
  • Higher token cost.
  • Lower answer precision.

Chunking solves this.

Instead of retrieving

Entire Book

RAG retrieves only

Relevant Sections

This produces

  • Smaller prompts
  • Lower token usage
  • Better retrieval precision
  • More focused answers

docs.title: Why Overlap?
docs.text:
Imagine

Chunk 1

Spring Boot provides dependency

-----------------------------

Chunk 2

injection, allowing loose coupling...

The concept

dependency injection

has been split. Similarity search may fail.

Overlap duplicates a small portion of adjacent chunks.

Example

Chunk 1

...dependency injection

Chunk 2

dependency injection allows...

Now either chunk contains sufficient context. Overlap preserves semantic continuity across chunk boundaries.

docs.title: How should Chunk Size be chosen?
docs.text: There is no universally correct value. Chunk size depends on several tradeoffs.

docs.title: LLM Context Window
docs.text: Smaller chunks reduce prompt size.

docs.title: Embedding Model
docs.text: Different embedding models capture semantic meaning differently. Some perform better with shorter chunks. Others preserve context better with larger chunks.

docs.title: Retrieval Precision
docs.text:
Large chunks

  • More context
  • Less precise retrieval

Small chunks

  • Better retrieval precision
  • Risk of insufficient context

docs.title: Token Cost
docs.text:
Large chunks

  • Higher token usage

Small chunks

  • Lower prompt cost

docs.title: Nature of Documents
docs.text:
Different document types require different chunking strategies.

Examples

docs.title: Python Code
docs.text:
Split after

  • classes
  • methods
  • functions

Not inside methods.

docs.title: Markdown
docs.text:
Split after

  • headings
  • sections

docs.title: HTML
docs.text:
Split after

  • DOM sections
  • headers

docs.title: Plain Text
docs.text: RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter is generally suitable.

docs.title: Metadata after Chunking
docs.text:
Before chunking

Document(
    page_content="...",
    metadata={
        "source": "...",
        "page": 5
    }
)

After chunking

Document(
    page_content="Smaller chunk...",
    metadata={
        "source": "...",
        "page": 5
    }
)

The Document type does not change. Only page_content becomes smaller. Metadata is inherited.

docs.title: Can Metadata become richer?
docs.text:
Yes. Additional metadata may be introduced.

Example

{
  "source": "spring.pdf",
  "page": 5,
  "chunk": 3,
  "section": "Dependency Injection",
  "language": "en"
}

This enables

  • filtering
  • tracing
  • debugging
  • citation
  • advanced retrieval

docs.title: Important Design Principle
docs.text:
Throughout the pipeline we continue passing

Document

Objects.

Only the contents evolve.

Document

↓

Loaded Document

↓

Chunked Document

↓

Embedded Document

↓

Stored Vector

The type remains consistent. This greatly simplifies downstream pipeline stages.

docs.title: Why RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter?
docs.text: It attempts to preserve semantic boundaries by recursively trying progressively smaller separators before finally splitting by character count. This creates more meaningful chunks than fixed-size character splitting.

docs.title: Why overlap?
docs.text: Overlap ensures important context near chunk boundaries is duplicated into adjacent chunks, preventing semantic information from being lost during retrieval.

docs.title: How do you choose chunk size?
docs.text:
Chunk size is a tradeoff between retrieval precision, context preservation, embedding quality, LLM context window, token cost, and the structure of the underlying documents.

Note

There is no universally optimal value.

docs.title: Biggest Takeaway
docs.text:
A production RAG pipeline is not about LangChain APIs. It is about information flowing through multiple well-separated stages.

Upload

↓

Load

↓

Chunk

↓

Embed

↓

Store

↓

Retrieve

↓

Generate

Each stage should have a single responsibility and expose a stable interface to the next stage.

docs.title: Design Patterns in our RAG Project (PR-3)
docs.text:
These notes capture the engineering decisions behind introducing the Strategy Pattern and Flyweight Pattern while building our RAG ingestion pipeline.

The goal is not to memorize design patterns. The goal is to understand why they naturally emerge while solving software engineering problems.

docs.title: PR-1
docs.text:
User

↓

UI

docs.title: PR-2
docs.text:
User

↓

Upload

↓

Loader Factory

↓

PdfLoader

↓

Documents

Introduced:

  • Factory Pattern
  • Polymorphism
  • Abstract Classes

docs.title: PR-3
docs.text:
User

↓

Upload

↓

Loader

↓

Documents

↓

Chunk Service

↓

Chunk Strategy

↓

Chunks

Introduced:

  • Strategy Pattern
  • Flyweight Pattern
  • Better Separation of Concerns

docs.title: Why did we need Strategy Pattern?
docs.text:
Initially our project only supported PDFs.

PDF

↓

RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

Everything seemed simple.

Then requirements changed.

The application should support

PDF

Markdown

Python

HTML

TXT

Question:

Should every file use

RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

?

Answer:

No.

Different document types require different chunking algorithms.

Examples

Document Type Preferred Chunking PDF RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter Markdown MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter Python PythonCodeTextSplitter HTML HTMLHeaderTextSplitter

The behavior varies. This is exactly what Strategy Pattern solves.

docs.title: Strategy Pattern
docs.text:
Instead of writing

if extension == ".pdf":
    ...

elif extension == ".py":
    ...

elif extension == ".md":
    ...

throughout the project, we encapsulate each algorithm inside its own class.

                 Strategy (Abstract)

                        ▲

      ┌─────────────────┼─────────────────┐

      │                 │                 │

      ▼                 ▼                 ▼

RecursiveStrategy  PythonStrategy  MarkdownStrategy

Every strategy exposes exactly the same interface.

chunk(document)

The caller never knows which implementation is executed.

docs.title: Why is this better?
docs.text:
Without Strategy

Chunk Service

↓

if PDF

↓

Recursive

↓

else if Python

↓

Python Splitter

↓

else if Markdown

Every new algorithm modifies existing code.

With Strategy

Chunk Service

↓

Strategy Factory

↓

Correct Strategy

↓

chunk(document)

The Chunk Service never changes.

docs.title: Factory vs Strategy
docs.text: This project uses both. They solve different problems.

docs.title: Factory
docs.text:
Question:

Which object should I create?

Example

Uploaded File

↓

LoaderFactory

↓

PdfLoader

Factory is responsible for object creation.

docs.title: Strategy
docs.text:
Question:

Which algorithm should execute?

Example

Document

↓

StrategyFactory

↓

RecursiveChunkStrategy

↓

chunk(document)

Strategy is responsible for behavior.

docs.title: They work together
docs.text:
Our architecture

Chunk Service

↓

Strategy Factory

↓

Strategy

↓

LangChain

The factory chooses the appropriate strategy. The strategy executes the algorithm.

docs.title: Chunk Service
docs.text:
Responsibility

  • Iterate over Documents
  • Ask Factory for appropriate strategy
  • Aggregate chunks

It should never

  • know chunking algorithms
  • perform splitting
  • inspect file types

Its only job is orchestration.

docs.title: Strategy Factory
docs.text:
Responsibility

Given

Document

Return

Correct Chunk Strategy

Example

.pdf

↓

RecursiveChunkStrategy

----------------------

.py

↓

PythonChunkStrategy

----------------------

.md

↓

RecursiveChunkStrategy

Notice

Multiple document types may reuse the same strategy. The factory chooses algorithms, not document types.

docs.title: Why choose strategy per Document?
docs.text:
Suppose user uploads

resume.pdf

main.py

README.md

Every document requires a different chunking algorithm.

Therefore

ChunkService

↓

for each Document

↓

StrategyFactory

↓

Correct Strategy

If we selected strategy only once,

mixed uploads would become impossible.

docs.title: Why use split_documents() instead of split_text()?
docs.text:
Initially

split_text(document.page_content)

was considered.

Problem

Metadata would be lost.

Instead

split_documents([document])

returns

List[Document]

Each chunk inherits

metadata

Example

Before

Document(
    page_content="...",
    metadata={
        "source":"spring.pdf",
        "page":7
    }
)

After chunking

Document(
    page_content="Smaller chunk",
    metadata={
        "source":"spring.pdf",
        "page":7
    }
)

Metadata survives automatically.

docs.title: Flyweight Pattern
docs.text:
After Strategy was implemented,

we noticed

StrategyFactory.create(document)

created

RecursiveChunkStrategy()

for every document.

Example

Document 1

↓

RecursiveChunkStrategy()

Document 2

↓

RecursiveChunkStrategy()

Document 3

↓

RecursiveChunkStrategy()

This is unnecessary. The strategy stores no state.

docs.title: Flyweight Solution
docs.text:
The Factory owns reusable strategy instances.

StrategyFactory

│

├── RecursiveChunkStrategy

├── PythonChunkStrategy

└── MarkdownChunkStrategy

Whenever requested

PDF

↓

same RecursiveChunkStrategy object

Multiple documents share one strategy object.

docs.title: Why Flyweight works
docs.text:
Our Strategy stores no mutable state.

chunk(document)

instead of

self.document

Therefore

the same object can safely process

PDF A

↓

PDF B

↓

PDF C

docs.title: When would Flyweight NOT work?
docs.text:
Suppose Strategy stores

self.chunk_size = 500

and another request changes it to

1000

Now, all users share the same mutable object. Unexpected behaviour appears. Flyweight requires shared objects to remain effectively immutable or stateless.

docs.title: Why we still implemented Flyweight
docs.text:
Production? Probably unnecessary. Creating one Strategy object is extremely cheap.

However, this project is primarily for learning. Implementing Flyweight helped understand - object sharing - stateless services - object lifecycle - memory optimisation patterns

docs.title: Future Improvement
docs.text:
Currently

.pdf

↓

Recursive Strategy

is hardcoded.

Future

chunking:

  pdf: recursive

  md: recursive

  py: python

  html: html

The Factory could read configuration instead of hardcoding mappings. No source code changes required.

docs.title: Single Responsibility Principle
docs.text: Chunk Service orchestrates. Strategy chunks. Factory creates strategies. Every class has one reason to change.

docs.title: Open/Closed Principle
docs.text:
Adding

SemanticChunkStrategy

requires

  • creating one new class
  • modifying only the Factory

Existing strategies remain untouched.

docs.title: Dependency Inversion
docs.text:
Chunk Service depends on

Strategy

not

RecursiveChunkStrategy

Concrete implementations remain hidden.

docs.title: Final Architecture
docs.text:
Upload

                    │

                    ▼

          Loader Factory

                    │

                    ▼

               Documents

                    │

                    ▼

             Chunk Service

                    │

                    ▼

          Strategy Factory

                    │

      ┌─────────────┴─────────────┐

      ▼                           ▼

RecursiveChunkStrategy    PythonChunkStrategy

      │                           │

      └─────────────┬─────────────┘

                    ▼

             LangChain Splitters

                    ▼

                 Chunks

docs.title: Biggest Takeaways
docs.text:
• Factory creates objects.
  • Strategy encapsulates algorithms.
  • Flyweight reuses stateless objects.
  • Services orchestrate workflows.
  • The pipeline continues carrying Document objects.
  • Metadata should never be discarded.
  • Choose the strategy at the smallest unit where behaviour varies.
  • Design patterns are not interview topics—they naturally emerge while solving engineering problems.

docs.title: Why did you introduce Strategy Pattern?
docs.text: Different document types require different chunking algorithms. Strategy encapsulates each algorithm behind a common interface, allowing the Chunk Service to remain independent of implementation details.

docs.title: Why use a Factory with Strategy?
docs.text: The Factory selects the appropriate strategy based on document metadata. The Strategy performs the chunking algorithm. Factory chooses which object, Strategy defines what behaviour.

docs.title: Why process one Document at a time?
docs.text: Different uploaded documents may require different chunking strategies. Selecting the strategy per Document allows mixed uploads (PDF, Markdown, Python) to be handled correctly.

docs.title: Why use split_documents()?
docs.text: It preserves LangChain Document objects along with metadata, enabling traceability, filtering, and downstream retrieval.

docs.title: Why implement Flyweight?
docs.text: Chunking strategies are stateless. Reusing a single strategy instance avoids unnecessary object creation and demonstrates efficient object sharing, although the optimization is primarily educational in this project.

docs.title: Biggest Engineering Lesson
docs.text:
Design patterns should never be introduced because they are famous. They should emerge naturally when solving real software engineering problems.

In this project: - Factory solved object creation. - Strategy solved algorithm variation. - Flyweight solved object reuse.

docs.title: PR-4 Discussion — Embedding Pipeline & Designing for PR-5
docs.text:
These notes capture the engineering decisions behind designing the Embedding Pipeline and how it naturally connects to the upcoming Vector Database layer.

The objective is not simply generating embeddings. The objective is understanding how responsibilities are distributed across services in a scalable RAG architecture.

docs.title: Current Architecture
docs.text:
After PR-3, the pipeline looks like:

Upload

↓

Loader Factory

↓

Documents

↓

Chunk Service

↓

Chunks (List<Document>)

PR-4 extends it to:

Upload

↓

Loader Factory

↓

Documents

↓

Chunk Service

↓

Chunks

↓

Embedding Service

↓

Embeddings

Notice that we still haven't introduced the Vector Database. Embeddings exist before they are stored.

docs.title: Why EmbeddingService?
docs.text:
Question:

Should ingestion.py directly call the embedding model?

Example

embedding_model.embed_documents(...)

Answer

No.

ingestion.py is an orchestration layer. It should coordinate the workflow but never know implementation details.

Its responsibility is simply

Load

↓

Chunk

↓

Embed

↓

Store

↓

Retrieve

This keeps the application extensible and follows the Single Responsibility Principle.

docs.title: Responsibility of EmbeddingService
docs.text:
EmbeddingService is responsible for

  • selecting the embedding model
  • generating embeddings
  • batching requests (future)
  • measuring embedding performance
  • hiding LangChain implementation details

It should not

  • know about Vector Databases
  • know about FAISS
  • know about retrieval
  • know about UI

Its only job is

Text

↓

Vectors

docs.title: Should Strategy Pattern be used?
docs.text:
Initially the answer seems yes because embedding models can vary.

Examples

BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5

↓

384 dimensions

-------------------------

OpenAI text-embedding-3-small

↓

1536 dimensions

-------------------------

Nomic Embed

↓

768 dimensions

Different models. Different dimensions. Different retrieval quality.

However

today, our application supports exactly one model.

Introducing Strategy now would create unnecessary abstraction.

Engineering lesson

Design for extension.

Implement for today's requirements.

Therefore

PR-4 intentionally keeps

Embedding Service

↓

Embedding Model

without introducing Strategy.

docs.title: When Strategy becomes useful
docs.text:
Suppose

Premium users

↓

OpenAI Embeddings

Free users

↓

HuggingFace Embeddings

Now

behavior varies.

At this point, Strategy Pattern naturally emerges.

The variation is

User Tier

not

Document Type

This is a key architectural insight. Always identify what actually varies before introducing Strategy.

docs.title: Why Factory is unnecessary here
docs.text:
During loading, different document types required different loader objects.

Factory solved

Which object should I create?

During embedding, every document is already represented as

Document

No object creation decision exists.

Therefore

Factory provides little value here.

docs.title: Service Contract
docs.text:
Question

What should EmbeddingService accept?

Answer

List[Document]

Question

What should it return?

EmbeddingService returns only

List[List[float]]

Its responsibility ends once vectors are produced.

docs.title: Why not return Vector Objects?
docs.text:
Suppose EmbeddingService returned

EmbeddingResult

{

document,

chunk,

metadata,

embedding

}

Question

Who actually needs this structure?

Answer

Only the Vector Database.

Therefore

EmbeddingService would be performing work that belongs elsewhere.

This violates proper responsibility allocation.

docs.title: Service Responsibilities
docs.text:
ChunkService

Input

List[Document]

Output

List[Document]

EmbeddingService

Input

List[Document]

Output

List[List[float]]

VectorStore

Input

documents,
embeddings

Output

Storage

Every service owns exactly one transformation.

docs.title: Relationship between Documents and Embeddings
docs.text:
Question

If EmbeddingService returns only vectors, how does the application know which embedding belongs to which Document?

Answer

Ordering.

Example

Documents

Chunk1

Chunk2

Chunk3

Embeddings

Vector1

Vector2

Vector3

Both lists preserve the same order.

Therefore

Chunk2

↓

Vector2

The relationship is naturally maintained.

docs.title: Where should mapping occur?
docs.text:
One proposal was introducing

EmbeddingResult

to combine

  • Document
  • Metadata
  • Chunk
  • Embedding

Although reasonable, only one component actually requires this information.

The Vector Database.

Therefore, the mapping responsibility belongs to

VectorStore

rather than EmbeddingService.

docs.title: Why VectorStore should perform mapping
docs.text:
VectorStore receives

documents

and

embeddings

Example

store(
    documents,
    embeddings
)

Internally

for document, embedding in zip(
    documents,
    embeddings
):

Store them together. This keeps EmbeddingService completely independent of storage implementation.

docs.title: Why use zip()?
docs.text:
Python provides

zip()

which iterates over two collections simultaneously.

Example

for document, embedding in zip(documents, embeddings):

Each embedding automatically corresponds to its respective Document. No intermediate DTO is required.

docs.title: Why doesn't EmbeddingService know FAISS?
docs.text:
EmbeddingService should not know

  • FAISS
  • Chroma
  • Pinecone
  • Qdrant

It simply converts

Chunk

↓

Vector

Storage concerns belong exclusively to the Vector Database layer.

docs.title: Does the Vector Database determine dimensions?
docs.text:
Embedding models determine vector dimensionality.

Examples

BAAI

↓

384

------------------

OpenAI

↓

1536

------------------

Nomic

↓

768

The Vector Database simply validates that every inserted vector has the expected dimension. It does not choose it.

Rule

One Vector Database index should contain vectors generated by a single embedding model (or models with the same output dimensionality and representation).

docs.title: Why don't we directly embed PDFs?
docs.text:
Embedding models operate on text not binary document formats.

Therefore, the pipeline becomes

PDF

↓

Loader

↓

Text

↓

Chunking

↓

Embeddings

Chunking is essential because embedding an entire document would

  • produce generic embeddings
  • reduce retrieval precision
  • increase token usage
  • waste context window
  • reduce answer quality

Instead, only semantically relevant chunks are retrieved during RAG.

docs.title: Future Pipeline (PR-5)
docs.text:
After EmbeddingService

the architecture becomes

Chunks

↓

Embedding Service

↓

Embeddings

↓

Vector Store

↓

Similarity Search

↓

Retrieved Chunks

↓

LLM

Notice, EmbeddingService finishes before retrieval begins.

docs.title: Information Expert Principle
docs.text:
A major design discussion occurred around who should combine Documents and Embeddings.

Conclusion: The component that owns storage should combine them.

This follows the GRASP principle Information Expert.

Give responsibility to the component that possesses the required knowledge.

docs.title: Final Architecture
docs.text:
Upload

↓

Loader Factory

↓

Documents

↓

Chunk Service

↓

Chunks

↓

Embedding Service

↓

Embeddings

↓

Vector Store

↓

Retriever

↓

LLM

docs.title: Single Responsibility
docs.text: Each service performs one transformation.

docs.title: Separation of Concerns
docs.text: Embedding generation remains independent from storage.

docs.title: Open/Closed Principle
docs.text: New embedding models can later be introduced without changing orchestration.

docs.title: Information Expert (GRASP)
docs.text: Responsibilities belong to the component that owns the required knowledge.

docs.title: Future Improvements
docs.text:
When multiple embedding providers are supported

introduce

Embedding Strategy

Example

Embedding Service

↓

Embedding Strategy

↓

OpenAI Strategy

↓

HuggingFace Strategy

↓

Nomic Strategy

Only then does Strategy become justified.

docs.title: Why introduce EmbeddingService?
docs.text: To isolate embedding logic from orchestration, making the application extensible while hiding implementation details.

docs.title: Why doesn't ingestion.py directly call LangChain?
docs.text: Because ingestion is responsible only for coordinating pipeline stages, not implementing them.

docs.title: Why not use Strategy immediately?
docs.text: Only one embedding model currently exists. Strategy should be introduced when multiple interchangeable algorithms exist.

docs.title: Why not return Vector Objects?
docs.text:
EmbeddingService should only transform text into vectors.

Storage structures belong to the Vector Database.

docs.title: Why preserve ordering?
docs.text:
The nth embedding always corresponds to the nth Document. The Vector Database can combine them using

zip(documents, embeddings)

without additional mapping objects.

docs.title: Why shouldn't EmbeddingService know FAISS?
docs.text: Embedding generation and storage are independent responsibilities. This keeps the architecture modular and allows swapping Vector Databases without changing embedding logic.

docs.title: Biggest Engineering Lesson
docs.text:
During this PR we realized that software architecture is not about creating more classes.

It is about giving every component exactly one responsibility.

The cleanest architecture often emerges by asking

"Who actually owns this responsibility?"

instead of

"Where can I put this code?"
